issue.title: discovery: listEvents omits `closed` by default — first-page result is dominated by settled events
issue.body:
`ListEventsRequestSchema` (`packages/client/src/actions/events.ts:34-75`) declares `closed: z.boolean().optional()`, and `toEventsSearchParams` drops keys whose value is `undefined`. So calling `client.listEvents({ pageSize: 10 })` sends no `closed` parameter to the Gamma `/events/keyset` endpoint — and the Gamma server in turn applies its own default, which is `closed=true` rather than `false`.

## Live evidence

```
$ curl -s 'https://gamma-api.polymarket.com/events?series_id=10345&limit=100' \
    | jq '[.[] | .closed] | group_by(.) | map({(.[0]|tostring): length}) | add'
{"true": 100}
```

(Reproduction borrowed from Polymarket/polymarket-cli#71, which reports the same default-mismatch propagating into the CLI. The CLI omits the parameter when neither `--closed` nor `--active` is set; the new SDKs do the same when callers omit `closed`.)

For a less-filtered query the bias is also visible:

```
$ curl -s 'https://gamma-api.polymarket.com/events?limit=50' \
    | jq '[.[] | .closed] | group_by(.) | map({(.[0]|tostring): length}) | add'
```

returns roughly two-thirds `closed=true`, even though "list events" is the natural thing a brand-new SDK user does when exploring the surface.

## Why this looks like a UX gap rather than a deliberate default

Both `listEvents` `@example` blocks (`packages/client/src/actions/events.ts:144-160`) pass `closed: false` explicitly:

```ts
const result = listEvents(client, {
  closed: false,
  pageSize: 10,
});
```

So the maintainer already knew that omitting `closed` produces the wrong result for the common "show me current events" workflow — the examples set it deliberately. New SDK users skimming the surface and calling `client.listEvents({ pageSize: 10 })` (the example without `closed`) instead surface settled-only events. The docstring doesn't explain the default behavior.

The public Gamma docs (https://docs.polymarket.com/developers/gamma-markets-api/get-events) say `closed` defaults to `false`, which conflicts with the server's actual behavior — so this might also be a server contract drift rather than purely an SDK doc gap.

## Possible directions

1. **Document the actual server default in the SDK** — add a TSDoc note on `closed?: boolean` in `ListEventsRequest` clarifying that omitting it returns settled events. Cheapest fix; doesn't change behavior.
2. **Apply a client-side default `closed: false`** in `listEvents` / `toEventsSearchParams` so the SDK behavior matches the Gamma docs and the example usage. Behavior-changing, but matches the "intent" the example already encodes. Might also be worth applying to `listMarkets` if the same default exists there.
3. **Server-side**: change the Gamma default to `false` so docs + server + SDK all agree. Out of scope for this repo but worth flagging back to the API team.

I'd lean (2) — the maintainer's own examples already use `closed: false`, so codifying that as the SDK default is consistent and removes a footgun for new users. But this is a contract decision that could go differently.

## Cross-SDK

Same pattern in py-sdk: `list_events(closed: bool | None = None)` (`src/polymarket/clients/async_public.py`, `clients/public.py`) → `list_events_spec` adds `closed` to params via `_add_optional` only when non-None → server applies its `closed=true` default. Filing companion issue at Polymarket/py-sdk.

(Also applies cross-repo to Polymarket/polymarket-cli#71, which is the original CLI report.)

Happy to PR (1) or (2) once you've decided which direction is right.

issue.body:
`list_events_spec` (`src/polymarket/_internal/actions/gamma.py:369+`) accepts `closed: bool | None = None` and forwards it to `_add_optional(params, "closed", closed)`, which only sets the query parameter when it's not `None`. ```

    | jq '[.[] | .closed] | group_by(.) | map({(.[0]|tostring): length}) | add'

issue.title: gamma: get_market(url=...) only accepts /market/{slug} URLs but Polymarket only serves /event/{slug}
issue.body:
`_parse_polymarket_url` in `src/polymarket/_internal/gamma_paths.py:88-97` requires the URL's first path segment to match the entity `kind` ("market" or "event"). For `get_market(url=...)`, that means `polymarket.com/market/<slug>`. Polymarket itself does not serve URLs in that shape: every market is reachable as `polymarket.com/event/<slug>` and `/market/<slug>` is a 307 to `/404`. So the only realistic URL a user can paste into `get_market(url=...)` is rejected.

## Verification

```
$ curl -sI 'https://polymarket.com/event/eth-updown-5m-1780069800' | head -1
HTTP/1.1 200 OK

$ curl -sI 'https://polymarket.com/market/eth-updown-5m-1780069800' | head -2
HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
location: /404

$ curl -sI 'https://polymarket.com/market/new-rhianna-album-before-gta-vi-926' | head -1
HTTP/1.1 307 Temporary Redirect
```

I tried both an event-aligned slug (`eth-updown-5m-...`, where market slug == event slug) and a market-specific slug from a multi-market event (`new-rhianna-album-before-gta-vi-926` under the `what-will-happen-before-gta-vi` event). Both 307 to `/404`.

## Current behavior

With the README's own example:

```python
# README.md:35
market: Market = client.get_market(url="https://polymarket.com/event/example-market")
```

trace:

```
_resolve_lookup("market", id=None, slug=None, url="https://polymarket.com/event/example-market")
  → ("url", "https://polymarket.com/event/example-market")
_parse_polymarket_url("https://polymarket.com/event/example-market", kind="market")
  → segments == ["event", "example-market"]
  → segments[0] != kind ("event" != "market")
  → raise UserInputError("Expected a Polymarket market URL.")
```

So the documented example throws the moment it runs.

The `_build_entity_path` API endpoint is `/markets/slug/<slug>` (gamma) regardless of which website path the slug came from — so the slug extraction itself is the only part the SDK needs from the URL.

## Possible directions

1. **Doc-only fix.** Change the README example to `polymarket.com/market/<slug>` to match what the parser currently accepts, even though that URL doesn't exist on Polymarket. Cheap, but only helps if you assume users construct URLs in this canonical form — they don't, they paste from the address bar.

2. **Accept event URLs for `get_market(url=...)` too.** Relax `_parse_polymarket_url` to accept `event` as a valid first segment for both `kind="market"` and `kind="event"`, since the public Polymarket URL structure is event-centric. Carries a subtlety: for a multi-market event (`/event/what-will-happen-before-gta-vi`), passing that URL to `get_market(url=...)` would 404 server-side because the event slug isn't a market slug — needs a clear error rather than silently extracting the wrong slug.

3. **Deprecate `url=` on `get_market`** and have users always pass `slug=` or `id=` directly (they can extract from any URL themselves). `get_event(url=...)` keeps the existing behavior since `/event/<event-slug>` does work end-to-end.

4. **Add a small redirect-resolver** that, given any Polymarket URL, asks the gamma API which entity it points to. Heavier; probably not worth it for SDK.

I think (2) or (3) is closer to what users actually need. Happy to PR (1) immediately as a low-risk band-aid that at least makes the documented example parse, while (2)/(3) is a separate larger conversation.

## Cross-SDK

ts-sdk has the same parser convention — `packages/client/src/actions/markets.ts:261` docstring example uses `polymarket.com/market/some-market-slug`, and the equivalent URL-shape check lives behind `prepareMarketLookup` / the markets actions there. Not filing on ts-sdk yet pending the design choice here, but the parity is intentional to flag.

issue.title: feat(auth): add builder api key management
issue.body:
Adds the missing builder API key management surface on secure clients.

- Add create/fetch/revoke auth actions for `/auth/builder-api-key`.
- Expose sync and async `SecureClient` methods.
- Parse fetched builder keys from either raw key strings or metadata records.

The revoke path uses `secure_clob`, so it is signed with L2 auth like `delete_api_key`.

Closes #51

Checked with:
- `.\.venv\Scripts\python.exe -m ruff check .`
- `.\.venv\Scripts\python.exe -m pyright`
- `.\.venv\Scripts\python.exe -m pytest -m "not integration"`
- `git diff --check`

<!-- CURSOR_SUMMARY -->
---

> [!NOTE]
> **Medium Risk**
> Touches authenticated credential management and secret-bearing API responses; scope is narrow and follows existing api-key patterns with tests.
> 
> **Overview**
> Adds **builder API key lifecycle** on authenticated CLOB clients: **create**, **list**, and **revoke** against `/auth/builder-api-key`, wired through internal auth actions and exposed on both `SecureClient` and `AsyncSecureClient` (mirroring existing user API key helpers).
> 
> **Create** returns a `BuilderApiKey` (key/secret/passphrase). **Fetch** returns `BuilderApiKeyInfo` rows; a new model accepts either plain key strings or objects with `createdAt` / `revokedAt`. **Revoke** uses `secure_clob` with L2-signed requests like `delete_api_key`. `BuilderApiKeyInfo` is re-exported from the public package. Unit tests cover parsers and transport paths.
> 
> <sup>Reviewed by [Cursor Bugbot](https://cursor.com/bugbot) for commit 3fd398d88082111199968a55c5045906c7e95938. Bugbot is set up for automated code reviews on this repo. Configure [here](https://www.cursor.com/dashboard/bugbot).</sup>

comment.body:
Original reporter here — went through the diff against the gap I described in #51. Two observations:

**Critical detail is handled correctly.** `revoke_builder_api_key` calls `secure_clob.delete_json("/auth/builder-api-key")`, and `test_revoke_builder_api_key_uses_secure_clob` asserts the `POLY_API_KEY` header is present on the DELETE. That's exactly the landmine flagged in #51 — ts-sdk's `revokeBuilderApiKey` (Polymarket/ts-sdk#68) uses the unauthenticated `client.clob` transport instead of the L2-signed `secureClob`. py-sdk now mirrors `delete_api_key` correctly.

**Improvement over the issue spec.** I had `fetch_builder_api_keys` returning `tuple[BuilderApiKey, ...]` in the suggested action; the PR instead introduces a separate `BuilderApiKeyInfo` model for the list response (just `key` / `created_at` / `revoked_at`) and keeps `BuilderApiKey` for the create response (with `secret` / `passphrase`). The list endpoint should never return secrets, so splitting the response shapes structurally avoids that footgun — better than my spec.

One nit: `BuilderApiKeyInfo.key` has a plain repr, whereas the existing `BuilderApiKey` (`polymarket.auth`) redacts `key` via `field(repr=False)` + a custom `__repr__`. The metadata `key` is a public identifier so the plain repr is defensible, but probably worth a deliberate call vs drift.

Otherwise LGTM as a parity addition.

issue.title: orders: fetch_builder_fee_rates lacks 404 handling — market order construction crashes on unregistered builder code
issue.body:
`fetch_builder_fee_rates` / `fetch_builder_fee_rates_sync` in `src/polymarket/_internal/actions/orders/market_data.py:75-92` issue `GET /fees/builder-fees/<code>` and don't translate or guard the 404 case. Callers in `src/polymarket/_internal/actions/orders/market.py:185-217` invoke them unconditionally whenever `params.builder_code` is set and non-zero, so a market-buy with an unregistered builder code crashes during fee resolution and never reaches order construction.

## Current code

```python
# src/polymarket/_internal/actions/orders/market_data.py:75-92
async def fetch_builder_fee_rates(ctx: AsyncClientContext, *, builder_code: str) -> BuilderFeeRates:
    validated = validate_builder_code(builder_code)
    if validated == BYTES32_ZERO:
        raise UserInputError(
            "builder_code must be a real builder; zero (0x000…000) represents no attribution."
        )
    data = await ctx.clob.get_json(f"/fees/builder-fees/{validated}")
    return BuilderFeeRates.parse_response(data)


def fetch_builder_fee_rates_sync(ctx: SyncClientContext, *, builder_code: str) -> BuilderFeeRates:
    validated = validate_builder_code(builder_code)
    if validated == BYTES32_ZERO:
        raise UserInputError(...)
    data = ctx.clob.get_json(f"/fees/builder-fees/{validated}")
    return BuilderFeeRates.parse_response(data)
```

```python
# src/polymarket/_internal/actions/orders/market.py:185-191
fee_info = await fetch_platform_fee_info(ctx, condition_id=condition_id)
builder_taker_fee_rate = Decimal(0)
if params.builder_code is not None and params.builder_code != BYTES32_ZERO:
    rates = await fetch_builder_fee_rates(ctx, builder_code=params.builder_code)
    builder_taker_fee_rate = rates.taker
return adjust_buy_amount_for_fees(...)
```

`ctx.clob.get_json` raises `RequestRejectedError(status=404)` on a missing builder code; the caller does not catch it and the entire market-order construction aborts.

## Live server behavior

```
$ curl -s -w '\n%{http_code}\n' \
    'https://clob.polymarket.com/fees/builder-fees/0xabcdef…cdef'
{"error":"builder code not found"}
404
```

The 404 is well-defined ("builder code not found"). CORS is `*` so this is a server-state issue, not a transport restriction.

## Same shape on clob-client-v2

Structurally the same as [Polymarket/clob-client-v2#51](<https://github.com/Polymarket/clob-client-v2/issues/51>) (ts/v2-transitional). [Polymarket/clob-client-v2#72](<https://github.com/Polymarket/clob-client-v2/issues/72>) by @Cassxbt proposes a silent try/catch fix — open since 2026-05-21 without maintainer engagement, so the design direction looks unresolved.

## Design question

Three approaches:

1. **Silent skip**: catch `RequestRejectedError` where `status == 404` in `fetch_builder_fee_rates`, return `BuilderFeeRates(maker=Decimal(0), taker=Decimal(0))`. Order proceeds without builder attribution — silently. Bad UX for the case the caller actually wanted attribution.
2. **Typed error mapping**: introduce `UnknownBuilderCodeError(RequestRejectedError)` (mirrors the existing pattern in `polymarket/_internal/eoa/rpc.py:13: class JsonRpcCallError(RequestRejectedError):`). `fetch_builder_fee_rates` re-raises 404 as `UnknownBuilderCodeError`; market-order callers can catch it and decide policy.
3. **Server-side**: distinguish unknown-vs-disabled / not-yet-propagated.

I'd lean (2) — preserves information for the caller and matches the existing typed-error pattern. But this is a design call and could go differently depending on what the platform contract for "unknown builder code" is meant to be.

## Impact

Limit orders are unaffected (they don't fetch fees in `place.py`). The break is scoped to **market orders with builder attribution** — `prepare_market_order_draft` and the `_resolve_market_buy_amount` / `_resolve_market_buy_amount_sync` paths via `market.py:185-217`. A builder who pastes a code that isn't yet registered (testing, propagation lag, copy/paste of wrong env) gets a `RequestRejectedError` from a `prepare_market_*` call rather than from a fee lookup, which is hard to debug.

## Cross-SDK

Same pattern in ts-sdk: Polymarket/ts-sdk#74 (`packages/client/src/actions/clob.ts:398-409` + `packages/client/src/actions/orders/market.ts:222-232`).

Happy to PR once you've picked a direction.

auth: beginAuthentication signs ClobAuth with EOA address, ignoring POLY_1271 deposit wallet shape
issue.body:
`PublicClient.beginAuthentication` in `packages/client/src/clients.ts:374-432` signs the `ClobAuth` EIP-712 payload and creates the API key using `signerAddress` (the EOA) for every wallet type, with no branch on `identity.walletType`. The same authentication path is taken for `WalletType.DEPOSIT_WALLET` (i.e. `SignatureType.POLY_1271`), which produces an API key that's incompatible with how the SDK later signs orders.

## Current flow

```typescript
// clients.ts:388-394
const signerAddress = expectEvmAddress(yield requestAddress());
const wallet = expectEvmAddress(params.wallet);
const identity = resolveAccountIdentity(
  this.environment,
  signerAddress,
  wallet,
);

// clients.ts:418-432
const signature = yield {
  kind: 'signAuthMessage',
  payload: createApiKeyAuthTypedDataPayload({
    address: signerAddress,            // ← EOA, regardless of walletType
    chainId: this.environment.chainId,
    nonce,
    timestamp,
  }),
};
const credentials = await createOrDeriveApiKey(this, {
  address: signerAddress,              // ← POLY_ADDRESS header = EOA
  nonce,
  signature: expectEvmSignature(signature),  // ← raw 65-byte ECDSA, no 1271 wrap
  timestamp,
});
```

`identity.walletType` is computed (lines 390-394 via `classifyWalletType` → `DEPOSIT_WALLET` / `GNOSIS_SAFE` / `POLY_PROXY` / `EOA`) but never consulted in the signing/header path.

## Asymmetry with the order path

The order path correctly branches on signature type:

```typescript
// packages/client/src/actions/orders/orders.ts:28-29
signer:
  signatureType === SignatureType.POLY_1271 ? account.wallet : order.signer,
```

And the typed-data builder wraps orders in `TypedDataSign` with the `DepositWallet` domain when `signatureType === POLY_1271` (`packages/client/src/actions/orders/typed-data.ts:108-139`). So **orders** authored by a deposit wallet sign as the wallet via ERC-7739 wrapping; **authentication** does not.

## Server-side consequence (matches clob-client-v2 cluster)

The API key is registered with `POLY_ADDRESS = <EOA>`. Later order submissions carry `maker = account.wallet` (the deposit wallet) and `signer = account.wallet` (per orders.ts:29). The CLOB's order-acceptance check ties the order's `signer` to the api-key holder, which now mismatch — same shape reported repeatedly on the V2 transitional client:

* [Polymarket/clob-client-v2#67](<https://github.com/Polymarket/clob-client-v2/issues/67>), Polymarket/ts-sdk#69, [#73](<https://github.com/Polymarket/ts-sdk/issues/73>), [#75](<https://github.com/Polymarket/ts-sdk/issues/75>) (deposit wallet orders rejected, "the order signer address has to be the address of the API KEY")
* [Polymarket/clob-client-v2#64](<https://github.com/Polymarket/clob-client-v2/issues/64>), Polymarket/ts-sdk#65, Polymarket/ts-sdk#66 (root cause: `createApiKey()` doesn't EIP-1271-wrap L1 auth)

Same root cause, different code organization.

## What's needed to fix

Two pieces, mirroring the order-signing path:

1. **POLY_ADDRESS / ClobAuth** `address` **should be the deposit wallet** when `identity.walletType === DEPOSIT_WALLET`, not `signerAddress`. The SDK already has `identity.wallet` in scope at line 389.
2. **The signature must be ERC-7739** `TypedDataSign`**-wrapped** so the CLOB server's `isValidSignature` call against the deposit wallet contract validates. The wrap pattern already exists for orders in `packages/client/src/actions/orders/typed-data.ts:108-139` (uses the `DepositWallet` domain with `verifyingContract: <funder>`, `salt: BYTES32_ZERO`, contents = the inner typed-data message).

The blocker is two server-side constants the SDK can't infer alone:

* The **app domain separator** the CLOB server uses when calling `isValidSignature` on the deposit wallet for the L1-auth flow (analogous to the CTF Exchange V2 domain in `_appDomainSeparator` for orders).
* The `CONTENTS_TYPE` **string** for `ClobAuth` (analogous to `_ORDER_TYPE_STRING`).

Once those are confirmed, the TS fix is straightforward — `expectEvmSignature` becomes the inner sig, and the trailer is built from the two constants in the same way `build_order_signature` does it in py-sdk's `typed_data.py:97-108`.

## Impact

Every user with a V2 deposit wallet hits this on first authentication. The credentials object returned by `createSecureClient` looks valid (it's a real api-key-creds tuple), but the first order submission gets rejected by the server. Users have no SDK-side signal that authentication produced an EOA-bound key, so the failure shows up only at order-placement time as a confusing "signer address has to be the address of the API KEY" error from the server.

## Cross-SDK

Same bug in py-sdk: `_internal/l1_auth.py:55-77` signs ClobAuth with `signer.address` (EOA) for all wallet types; `clients/async_secure.py:2360` does not pass / check `wallet_type` into `sign_api_key_auth`. Filing companion issue on py-sdk.

Happy to send a PR once external PRs are accepted and the two server-side constants are confirmed.

issue.title: auth: sign_api_key_auth uses signer.address for ClobAuth, ignoring POLY_1271 deposit wallet shape
issue.body:
`sign_api_key_auth` in `src/polymarket/_internal/l1_auth.py:55-77` signs the `ClobAuth` EIP-712 payload using `signer.address` (the EOA) for every wallet type, and `_resolve_api_credentials` in `src/polymarket/clients/async_secure.py:2360-2363` calls it without passing `wallet_type` or any deposit-wallet context. The resulting API key is registered against the EOA, which is incompatible with how the SDK later signs orders for `WalletType.DEPOSIT_WALLET` (`SignatureType.POLY_1271 = 3`).

## Current flow

```python
# _internal/l1_auth.py:55-77
def sign_api_key_auth(
    signer: LocalAccount,
    *,
    chain_id: int,
    timestamp: int,
    nonce: int = 0,
) -> ApiKeyAuthSignature:
    typed_data = build_api_key_auth_typed_data(
        address=signer.address,        # ← EOA, regardless of wallet_type
        chain_id=chain_id,
        timestamp=timestamp,
        nonce=nonce,
    )
    try:
        signed = signer.sign_typed_data(full_message=typed_data)  # ← raw ECDSA, no 1271 wrap
    except Exception as error:
        raise SigningError(...) from error
    return ApiKeyAuthSignature(
        address=signer.address,        # ← POLY_ADDRESS header = EOA
        nonce=nonce,
        signature="0x" + signed.signature.hex(),
        timestamp=timestamp,
    )
```

```python
# clients/async_secure.py:2360-2363
signature = sign_api_key_auth(
    signer, chain_id=environment.chain_id, timestamp=int(time.time()), nonce=nonce
)
return await _auth_actions.create_or_derive_api_key(clob, signature)
```

The caller does not consult `wallet_type` (despite `_ctx.wallet_type` being available elsewhere, e.g. `async_secure.py:1928, 1953, 1988, 2014`).

## Asymmetry with the order path

The order path already implements the POLY_1271 wrap correctly:

* `src/polymarket/_internal/actions/orders/orders.py:20` — `signer = wallet if signature_type == _POLY_1271_SIGNATURE_TYPE else draft.signer`
* `src/polymarket/_internal/actions/orders/typed_data.py:65-90` — `build_order_typed_data` wraps the order in `TypedDataSign` with the `DepositWallet` domain (`_DEPOSIT_WALLET_DOMAIN_NAME = "DepositWallet"`, `verifyingContract: order.signer`, `salt: BYTES32_ZERO`) for `_POLY_1271_SIGNATURE_TYPE`.
* `src/polymarket/_internal/actions/orders/typed_data.py:93-108` — `build_order_signature` builds the ERC-7739 trailer (`appDomainSep + contentsHash + CONTENTS_TYPE hex + length_BE2`).

So **orders** authored by a deposit wallet sign via ERC-7739 wrapping with the wallet as `signer`/maker; **authentication** still signs with the EOA. The auth path was simply not updated when the order path picked up POLY_1271 support.

## Server-side consequence (matches clob-client-v2 cluster)

The API key is registered with `POLY_ADDRESS = <EOA>`. Later orders carry `maker = ctx.wallet` (deposit wallet) and the `signer` field set to the wallet (per `orders.py:20`). The CLOB's acceptance check ties the order's `signer` to the api-key holder, which now mismatch — same shape reported repeatedly on the V2 transitional client:

* [Polymarket/clob-client-v2#64](<https://github.com/Polymarket/clob-client-v2/issues/64>), [#65](<https://github.com/Polymarket/py-sdk/issues/65>), [#66](<https://github.com/Polymarket/py-sdk/issues/66>) (root cause: `createApiKey()` doesn't EIP-1271-wrap L1 auth)
* [Polymarket/clob-client-v2#67](<https://github.com/Polymarket/clob-client-v2/issues/67>), [#69](<https://github.com/Polymarket/py-sdk/issues/69>), [#73](<https://github.com/Polymarket/py-sdk/issues/73>), [#75](<https://github.com/Polymarket/py-sdk/issues/75>) (deposit wallet orders rejected with "the order signer address has to be the address of the API KEY")

Same root cause, different code organization.

## What's needed to fix

Two pieces, mirroring `build_order_typed_data` / `build_order_signature`:

1. `ApiKeyAuthSignature.address` **and the** `address` **field in the ClobAuth typed-data message must be the deposit wallet** when `wallet_type == "DEPOSIT_WALLET"`, not `signer.address`. Pass `wallet` / `wallet_type` from `_resolve_api_credentials` into `sign_api_key_auth`.
2. **The signature must be ERC-7739** `TypedDataSign`**-wrapped** so the CLOB server's `isValidSignature` call on the deposit wallet contract validates. The wrap pattern already exists in `typed_data.py:65-108` and can be ported to a `_build_api_key_auth_typed_data` deposit-wallet variant + `_build_api_key_auth_signature` trailer builder.

The blocker is two server-side constants the SDK can't infer alone:

* The **app domain separator** the CLOB server uses when calling `isValidSignature` on the deposit wallet for the L1-auth flow (analogous to `_app_domain_separator(order)` for orders, which derives from the CTF Exchange V2 domain).
* The `CONTENTS_TYPE` **string** for `ClobAuth` (analogous to `_ORDER_TYPE_STRING`).

Once those two are confirmed by Polymarket, the py-sdk implementation is mechanical — the existing `typed_data.py` infrastructure can be parametrized over the contents-type.

## Impact

Every user with a V2 deposit wallet hits this on first authentication. The `ApiKeyCreds` returned looks valid, but the first order submission fails server-side with the "signer address has to be the address of the API KEY" error.

## Cross-SDK

Same bug in ts-sdk: Polymarket/ts-sdk#73 (`beginAuthentication` in `packages/client/src/clients.ts:418-432` signs `ClobAuth` with `signerAddress` regardless of `identity.walletType`, with no wrap).

Happy to send a PR once the two server-side constants are confirmed.

comment.body:
Quick FYI: I have a candidate fix on a fork branch — `BaseClient` → `BaseSecureClient`, `client.clob` → `client.secureClob`, two-line port from `deleteApiKey` plus a patch-level changeset. Branch: https://github.com/Nexory/ts-sdk/tree/fix/revoke-builder-api-key-l2-auth

I attempted to open it as a PR but `CreatePullRequest` 404s on this repo (looks like external-PR interaction-limits — `gh repo view Polymarket/ts-sdk --json allow_forking` is fine, fork exists, branch is `ahead_by: 1, behind_by: 0`, but the POST is rejected; the same fork-and-PR flow worked on Polymarket/py-sdk for other contributors).

If you'd prefer to ship this as your own follow-up (matching how #64 / #65 / #66 / #69 were addressed via maintainer PRs #45 / #46), no action needed from me beyond the link above. Happy to also send it through any contributor flow you'd prefer.

issue.title: createApiKey() / create_or_derive_api_key() doesn't EIP-1271-wrap L1 auth for POLY_1271 deposit wallets — orders rejected with signer != api_key

comment.body:
I dug into this and wanted to add a concrete code-trace through the current TS SDK and an architectural pointer that should save whoever picks this up some time. Posting here in case it's useful for the next maintainer pass; happy to follow up with a PR once a few server-side details are confirmed (see end).

### Current TS auth path (1.0.5)

1. `ClobClient.createApiKey()` / `deriveApiKey()` (`src/client.ts:498` / `:524`) call `createL1Headers(this.signer, this.chainId, nonce, ts)` — they do **not** pass `this.funderAddress` or `this.signatureType`.
2. `createL1Headers` (`src/headers/index.ts:15`) accepts an optional 5th `address` parameter and forwards it to `buildClobEip712Signature`, then sets `POLY_ADDRESS = address ?? getSignerAddress(signer)`.
3. `buildClobEip712Signature` (`src/signing/eip712.ts:12`) calls `signTypedDataWithSigner` (`src/signing/signer.ts`) on the EOA signer for the `ClobAuthDomain` / `ClobAuth` typed data — with no `signatureType` branch.

So the gap is in two places, not one: even if `createApiKey` were patched to pass `funderAddress` as the 5th `address` arg, `buildClobEip712Signature` would still produce a plain ECDSA signature recovering the EOA, and the resulting `POLY_SIGNATURE` would not validate as an ERC-1271 signature for the deposit-wallet `POLY_ADDRESS`. Both (a) params and (b) signing have to be fixed.

### What the Rust SDK appears to do

In `Polymarket/rs-clob-client-v2`, the inner header-building function (`src/auth.rs::l1::create_headers`) takes `(signer, chain_id, ts, nonce)` and signs `ClobAuth` via `signer.sign_hash()` — i.e. the inner function looks essentially the same as the TS one. The deposit-wallet behavior must therefore live one layer up: the `AuthenticationBuilder` returned by `Client::authentication_builder(...)` consumes `.funder(...)` and `.signature_type(...)` and presumably wraps the user-supplied `Signer` into a composite signer whose `address()` returns the funder and whose `sign_hash()` returns an ERC-1271-compatible signature. That seems to be the only way the same inner `create_headers` can cleanly serve both EOA and POLY_1271 paths — but the full `authenticate()` body in `src/clob/client.rs` would confirm.

### Suggested TS architecture (mirrors that pattern, minimal-touch)

Rather than special-casing `signatureType` inside `buildClobEip712Signature`, introduce a small `FunderSigner` that wraps the user's `ClobSigner` when `signatureType === POLY_1271` + `funderAddress` are set:

- `getAddress()` returns `funderAddress` (the deposit wallet) — so existing `getSignerAddress(signer)` calls in `createL1Headers` and `buildClobEip712Signature` automatically pick that up.
- `signTypedData(domain, types, value, primaryType)` produces the ERC-7739 `TypedDataSign`-wrapped signature using the same envelope as `exchangeOrderBuilderV2.buildOrderSignature` (`src/order-utils/exchangeOrderBuilderV2.ts:148`), just with `ClobAuth`-shaped contents:

```
innerSig = sign( TypedDataSign{
  contents: ClobAuth { address, timestamp, nonce, message },
  name: "DepositWallet", version: "1",
  chainId, verifyingContract: funder, salt: 0x00...00,
} )
return  0x{innerSig}{appDomainSep}{contentsHash}{toHex(CONTENTS_TYPE)}{uint16_BE(len)}
```

Then `createApiKey` / `deriveApiKey` build a `FunderSigner` when `this.signatureType === POLY_1271 && this.funderAddress`, call `createL1Headers(funderSigner, …)` with no other changes, and `POLY_ADDRESS` becomes the funder automatically via `getSignerAddress(funderSigner)`. The rest of the L1-auth pipeline keeps working unchanged.

### Two server-side constants needed to finish a PR

The two values that make this mechanical-mirroring rather than guessing are:

- **`appDomainSep`** for the `ClobAuth` L1-auth flow — i.e. the EIP-712 domain separator the CLOB server uses when it calls `isValidSignature` on the deposit wallet for L1 auth (in the order path this is the CTF Exchange V2 domain).
- **`CONTENTS_TYPE`** string for `ClobAuth` (analogous to `ORDER_TYPE_STRING`).

Both are server-side configuration, not derivable from this repo. Once a maintainer confirms them (or points to where they live), the TS implementation is straightforward.

### Migration-path note

The new unified `Polymarket/ts-sdk` (which the README of this repo now recommends) has `SignatureType.POLY_1271` and `funder` references in `packages/client/src/wallet.ts`, but a code search for `createApiKey` / `deriveApiKey` / `L1Headers` / api-key methods in that repo returns no results — so deposit-wallet users on TS currently have **no migration target** either. They're stuck on this SDK until either this issue is fixed here or the unified ts-sdk grows api-key creation. Worth flagging on the unified-SDK side too.

Happy to put up the PR once the two server-side constants are confirmed.

Thanks — intentionally kept as-is for readability. The denom set is built in a separate phase from the length/excess check to keep both intents clear, and chainLimit is typically 1–5 entries so the extra iteration is negligible.

issue.title: fix(utils): correct self-funded v0.6 verification gas multiplier (0 -> 1)
issue.body:
Fixes #188.

`calculateUserOperationMaxGasCost` used a verification-gas multiplier of `0n` when no paymaster is set, which drops the `verificationGasLimit` term entirely from the **v0.6** prefund formula.

```diff
- const mul = isPaymasterAndData ? 3n : 0n;
+ const mul = isPaymasterAndData ? 3n : 1n;
```

EntryPoint v0.6 `_getRequiredPrefund` uses `mul = mUserOp.paymaster != address(0) ? 3 : 1`, so the no-paymaster case must be `1`. With `0n`, a self-funded v0.6 op's max gas cost was under-estimated (e.g. `verificationGasLimit=500k`, `maxFeePerGas=10M` → `1.6e12` reported vs `6.6e12` correct, ≈4.1×). The v0.7+ branch already sums all components correctly.

Downstream, this also under-estimates `CandidePaymaster`'s ERC-20 approve amount when the cost is computed before `paymasterAndData` is set, which can revert the operation on-chain.

### Tests
Adds `test/utils/calculateUserOperationMaxGasCost.test.js` covering the self-funded (mul=1) and paymaster (mul=3) v0.6 cases.

issue.title: fix(Coin): align fromString denom regex with Cosmos denom spec
issue.body:
Fixes #167.

`Coin.fromString` used the denom character class `[0-9a-zA-Z/]`, which is both narrower and looser than the Cosmos SDK denom spec (`[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9/:._-]{2,127}`):

- **Round-trip break:** denoms containing `:` `.` `_` `-` (e.g. tokenfactory subdenoms like `factory/<addr>/my-token`) failed to parse, so `Coin.fromString(coin.toString())` threw instead of round-tripping.
- **Silent mis-parse:** numeric-only strings matched and the trailing digits were treated as the denom — `Coin.fromString('5000')` returned `Coin('0', 500)` instead of throwing the documented error.

### Change

```diff
- const m = str.match(/^(-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?)([0-9a-zA-Z/]+)$/)
+ const m = str.match(/^(-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?)([a-zA-Z][0-9a-zA-Z/:._-]*)$/)
```

The denom group now requires a leading letter and allows the full Cosmos denom charset. Existing denoms (`uinit`, `ibc/<hash>`, decimal amounts) parse identically — verified against the current `fromString` tests.

### Tests

- round-trip parse of a denom containing `-` (`factory/init1abc/my-token`), including `fromString(coin.toString())`;
- numeric-only strings (`'5000'`, `'12'`) now throw.

issue.title: Configured discount percentages are validated and returned but do not appear applied in fee calculations
issue.body:
### Summary

`gasDiscountPercent` and `capitalDiscountPercent` are accepted as `RelayFeeCalculatorConfig` options, validated in the constructor, and returned in `RelayerFeeDetails`, but they do not appear to be applied to any of the computed fee values. Configuring a non-zero discount therefore has no observable effect on `gasFeeTotal`, `capitalFeeTotal`, or `relayFeeTotal`.

I'm not sure whether this is an oversight (the discount is meant to reduce the fee) or intended (the fields are informational and applied by the caller). Opening this to clarify the intended behavior.

### What I observed

In `src/relayFeeCalculator/relayFeeCalculator.ts`:

- Config options `gasDiscountPercent?` / `capitalDiscountPercent?` (interface, ~L69–70).
- Stored and validated in the constructor (~L157–167): `this.gasDiscountPercent = config.gasDiscountPercent || 0`, with `assert(this.gasDiscountPercent >= 0 && this.gasDiscountPercent <= 100, "gasDiscountPercent must be between 0 and 100 percent")` (and the analogous check for `capitalDiscountPercent`).
- Returned in `RelayerFeeDetails` (~L595 / L601), along with `auxNativeDiscountPercent` (~L561, L598) which is assigned from `this.gasDiscountPercent`.

However, the fee computations don't appear to reference them:

- `gasFeeTotal = gasFeePercent.mul(outToInDecimals(outputAmount)).div(fixedPointAdjustment)` (~L548)
- `capitalFeeTotal = capitalFeePercent.mul(outToInDecimals(outputAmount)).div(fixedPointAdjustment)` (~L555)
- `relayFeePercent` / `relayFeeTotal` are sums of the above, with no discount term (~L563–564)
- `gasFeePercent`, `capitalFeePercent`, and `auxNativeFeePercent` don't reference the discount fields either.

A repo-wide search shows the only references to `gasDiscountPercent` / `capitalDiscountPercent` are the config interface, the stored private fields, the constructor validation, and the echoed output — none inside a fee calculation. I didn't find a test that sets a non-zero discount and asserts a reduced fee, nor documentation describing the fields as pass-through.

### Question

Is the intent that these discounts are applied to the returned fees, or that they're informational (applied downstream by the caller)?

- If they're meant to reduce the fee, the application looks like it may be missing.
- If they're informational, validating them as `0–100` calculator config and returning them without applying them may be a little misleading — documenting that they're pass-through (or removing them) would make the contract clearer.

Happy to open a PR once the intended behavior is confirmed.

issue.title: calculateUserOperationMaxGasCost under-estimates self-funded v0.6 gas cost (verification multiplier is 0 instead of 1)
issue.body:
### Summary
`calculateUserOperationMaxGasCost` (`src/utils.ts`) under-estimates the max gas cost for **self-funded v0.6** UserOperations: the verification-gas multiplier is `0n` instead of `1n`, which drops the `verificationGasLimit` term entirely.

### Details
```ts
// src/utils.ts (v0.6 branch, selected via `"initCode" in useroperation`)
const mul = isPaymasterAndData ? 3n : 0n;
const requiredGas =
    useroperation.callGasLimit +
    useroperation.verificationGasLimit * mul +
    useroperation.preVerificationGas;
```

The EntryPoint v0.6 `_getRequiredPrefund` uses:
```solidity
uint256 mul = mUserOp.paymaster != address(0) ? 3 : 1;
uint256 requiredGas = mUserOp.callGasLimit + mUserOp.verificationGasLimit * mul + mUserOp.preVerificationGas;
```

With no paymaster the correct multiplier is **1**, not 0. Using `0n` makes `verificationGasLimit * 0 = 0`, dropping the entire verification-gas term for self-funded operations.

Example (`callGasLimit=100k`, `verificationGasLimit=500k`, `preVerificationGas=60k`, `maxFeePerGas=10M`, no paymaster):
- current: `100k + 0 + 60k = 160k` → `1.6e12` wei
- correct: `100k + 500k + 60k = 660k` → `6.6e12` wei (≈4.1× higher)

The v0.7+ branch (the `else` path) is correct — it sums all gas components.

### Impact
The reported max gas cost for self-funded v0.6 UserOperations is too low, so consumers using it for prefund/balance budgeting under-provision. It also feeds `CandidePaymaster`'s ERC-20 token-cost / approve-amount calculation; if invoked while `paymasterAndData` is still `"0x"`, the approve amount is under-estimated and the on-chain gas payment can exceed it, reverting the operation. (Fails closed — no fund loss.)

### Fix
`src/utils.ts`: change `const mul = isPaymasterAndData ? 3n : 0n;` to `... ? 3n : 1n;`. A PR is attached with the fix plus tests for both the self-funded (mul=1) and paymaster (mul=3) v0.6 cases.

issue.title: Grant comparison: chain grant missing a required denom counts as a match; Stake maxTokens compared by reference
issue.body:
Two correctness issues in `compareChainGrantsToTreasuryGrants` / `isLimitValid` (`packages/abstraxion-core/src/utils/grant/compare.ts`). Both affect whether an existing on-chain grant is judged to match the requested (treasury) configuration, which gates whether the SDK re-requests a grant. Neither is an authorization bypass — the chain enforces the actual grant — but both lead to incorrect re-grant decisions.

### 1. `isLimitValid` does not require all expected denoms to be present

`isLimitValid(expected, chain)` iterates only over `chain`, checking that no chain denom exceeds its expected cap and that there are no unexpected denoms. It does not verify that every `expected` denom is present in `chain`, so a chain grant that omits a required denom is treated as valid:

```ts
isLimitValid(
  [{ denom: 'uxion', amount: '1000' }, { denom: 'atom', amount: '500' }], // expected
  [{ denom: 'uxion', amount: '800' }],                                     // chain (no atom)
) // => true   (expected: false)
```

Via the `Send` branch of `compareChainGrantsToTreasuryGrants` (and the `MaxFunds` / `CombinedLimit` contract-execution branches, which also delegate to `isLimitValid`), a narrower on-chain `SendAuthorization` is counted as matching the broader treasury config, so the SDK skips re-requesting the full grant. A later transaction needing the omitted denom then fails on-chain.

### 2. Stake `maxTokens` compared by reference

In the `Stake` branch, `maxTokens` (a `Coin | undefined` object) is compared with `===`:

```ts
treasuryStakeAuth.maxTokens === grantStakeAuth.maxTokens
```

`allowList` / `denyList` on the surrounding lines use `JSON.stringify`, but `maxTokens` uses reference equality, which is never true for two independently decoded objects. Stake grants with a `maxTokens` set are therefore always classified as a mismatch, triggering an unnecessary re-grant.

### Fix

1. After the chain loop in `isLimitValid`, require every expected denom to be present on-chain.
2. Compare `maxTokens` by value (`?.denom` / `?.amount`, preserving the `undefined` case).

A PR is attached. The existing `isLimitValid` unit tests (which use full-coverage cases) are unaffected; a missing-denom regression test is added.

issue.title: Coin.fromString rejects valid Cosmos denoms (:._-) and silently mis-parses numeric-only strings
issue.body:
### Summary

`Coin.fromString` (and therefore `Coins.fromString`) uses a denom character class that is both narrower and looser than the Cosmos SDK denom specification:

```ts
// src/core/Coin.ts
const m = str.match(/^(-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?)([0-9a-zA-Z/]+)$/)
```

The Cosmos SDK denom regex is `[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9/:._-]{2,127}`, which leads to two issues.

### 1. Round-trip break for denoms containing `:` `.` `_` `-`

These characters are valid in Cosmos denoms (e.g. tokenfactory subdenoms such as `factory/<addr>/my-token`). `toString()` emits the denom verbatim, but `fromString` cannot parse it back:

```ts
const c = new Coin('factory/init1abc/my-token', 100)
Coin.fromString(c.toString())
// throws: "failed to parse to Coin: 100factory/init1abc/my-token"
```

So `Coin.fromString(coin.toString())` is not a reliable round-trip for such denoms.

### 2. Numeric-only strings are silently mis-parsed instead of throwing

Because the denom class allows digits, a string with no letter denom still matches and the trailing digit(s) are treated as the denom:

```ts
Coin.fromString('5000') // => Coin('0', 500)   (expected: throw)
Coin.fromString('12')   // => Coin('2', 1)
```

Callers such as `Coins.fromString` rely on the documented throw (`failed to parse to Coin: ...`) to detect malformed input, but instead receive a garbage coin that propagates unchecked.

### Suggested fix

Align the denom group with the Cosmos SDK spec — require the denom to start with a letter and allow `:._-`:

```ts
const m = str.match(/^(-?[0-9]+(\.[0-9]+)?)([a-zA-Z][0-9a-zA-Z/:._-]*)$/)
```

This fixes both cases and does not change parsing of existing denoms (`uinit`, `ibc/<hash>`, decimal amounts) — verified against the current `fromString` test cases. I'll open a PR with the one-line fix plus tests.
